data.title: The inevitable trend of the rise of equity tokens
data.description:
Author: MattyTranslator: Jiahuan, ChainCatcher

In November 2025, more than 5 years after the $UNI airdrop, Uniswap finally activated its fee switch.

This process was delayed for several years and involved repeated governance battles, even witnessing an extremely embarrassing scene in 2024: a 'stakeholder' (widely believed to be an equity investor) blocked a proposal that should have rewarded token holders. Despite this, the UNIFICation proposal ultimately passed with over 62 million votes.

The fact that Uniswap, as the largest DEX in the crypto space, has taken so long to figure out how to reward its token holders speaks volumes about the current state of equity versus token relations. While UNI token holders theoretically "own" the protocol, they can only watch from the sidelines as equity investors reap all the value from front-end fees. While Uniswap is a prime example of this equity versus token divergence, this problem has been festering for years, affecting virtually every protocol that consistently generates revenue. Equity holders and token holders frequently compete for the same value pool while operating under fundamentally different legal, governance, and economic frameworks. The solutions proposed within the industry are varied: from completely eliminating equity and transferring all ownership on-chain to going to the other extreme—completely abandoning tokens. Both approaches have their supporters, but also significant drawbacks. Extreme Path One: Complete De-equityization Completely eliminating equity and transferring all ownership concepts on-chain is undoubtedly a theoretical solution. In this vision, smart contracts replace shareholder agreements, on-chain balances replace equity structure tables, and governance tokens replace board voting. Instant settlement. Transparent ownership. What's not to like? A major problem is: unless a company's assets, operations, and customers are entirely on-chain, off-chain court systems will always be the ultimate arbitrators in resolving disputes. You can try to make all your off-chain contracts and protocols reference on-chain logic, but this still doesn't change the fact that off-chain courts are arbitrators, and not everything is within your control and can be transferred to on-chain. For example, I could have a tokenized real estate NFT issued by a smart contract claiming ownership of the property, but if the off-chain title deed for that land differs, good luck presenting your NFT when the sheriff delivers the eviction notice. (Again, you can take steps to try and ensure that the off-chain title deed matches the on-chain state, but this doesn't negate the fact that off-chain enforcement has priority.) The "no equity, pure token" approach only applies to a small subset of projects: Completely on-chain networks and protocols, such as Bitcoin, some public chains, and fully autonomous DeFi. These projects have no companies, no employees, no servers, and no external dependencies. After all, that's the beauty of Bitcoin in its original form! An uncensorable system and unconfiscable assets. However, this is not feasible for the vast majority of projects (and the vast majority of potential on-chain activity). Web2 and Web2.5 companies own off-chain assets, customers, payments, and operations. Extreme Path Two: Complete Detoxification At the other extreme of the spectrum, some projects (in fact, the vast majority of companies) have decided to completely abandon tokens. They raise equity, build products, and avoid all the headaches that tokens can bring—while sacrificing all the benefits. Benefits: No tokens mean no SEC knocking on your door. No need to worry about whether governance tokens are securities. No need to design token economics, worry about emissions, or explain buyback mechanisms. Costs: Giving up instant settlement, transparent ownership records, cost-efficiency benefits, and the ability to coordinate global community incentives. Traditional equity transfers are expensive, slow to settle, and inaccessible to most potential investors. Gaining equity exposure in private startups remains expensive, inefficient, and opaque. Even in 2026, the processes required to trade public stocks will seem outdated compared to DeFi. Tokens, despite their shortcomings, have the potential to solve these problems. They enable community ownership and user-owned products. To abandon this entirely would be a step backward. To find the optimal balance between these two extremes, we need to understand what equity offers that tokens cannot. What do equity and tokens each offer? 1. Legal Rights and Recourse When you own equity, you have legal standing. You can sue and enforce your rights. If directors breach fiduciary duty or commit fraud, you have an established legal framework to recover your losses. Token holders (with very few exceptions) have virtually no legally recognized rights or protections. They often have to rely on the market to save their investments. While theoretically a company's entire budget can be placed on-chain, allowing founders to delegate every decision to shareholders without legal authorization introduces significant operational inefficiencies and contradicts the initial purpose of investment—trusting the team's vision and capabilities. 2. Formal Governance Control Shareholders elect the board of directors, approve major transactions, and have the right to codify documents. In contrast, governance tokens often create an illusion of control. As Vitalik stated, token governance has serious flaws: low voting rates (<10%), whale manipulation, and a lack of expertise. More often than not, on-chain governance degenerates into a "decentralized theater," where teams can often ignore votes if they don't like the outcome, as execution still requires manual intervention. 3. Legal Clarity of Value Accumulation In mergers and acquisitions, equity holders have a clear legal right to receive profits. As recent cases with Tensor and Axelar have shown, token holders are often completely abandoned, even after the project has been acquired. Due to this strong legal right to profit sharing, stocks are more reliably traded based on multiples of expected future profits. Token valuations, on the other hand, are often purely speculative and lack fundamental support. Even if a project generates revenue, most projects do not reliably route that revenue to token holders due to regulatory risks and fiduciary duty conflicts. While off-chain protocols can be built to simulate this right, this is far less reliable than the legal basis of equity. 4. A Broader and Deeper Investor Pool In short, the equity market has a much larger investor pool and greater total purchasing power than the token market. The value of the US stock market alone is more than 20 times that of the entire crypto industry. The value of the global stock market is more than 46 times that of the crypto industry. Projects that choose tokens over equity are actually only reaching 2%-5% of the potential purchasing power they could otherwise access. 2026: The Year of Equity Tokens One thing is certain: from tokenized equity to new forms of on-chain governance, 2026 will be a year of innovation and experimentation with equity tokens. The DTC pilot project (launching in the second half of 2026) will be the first time in the US that participants are allowed to hold tokenized securities interests on a blockchain. This represents a shift of the backbone of the US capital market infrastructure onto the blockchain: Nasdaq has proposed trading tokenized securities. Securitize offers real publicly traded shares with full on-chain legal ownership. Centrifuge and others are tokenizing equity through SEC-registered agents. The integration of traditional financial infrastructure with the blockchain track is no longer a pipe dream—it's happening. For crypto-native projects, Uniswap's five-year journey toward a fee switch serves as a warning. The equity vs. token split won't resolve itself automatically. It requires deliberate design, clear protocols, and structures to resolve conflicts of interest. Ultimately, this divergence stems from regulatory uncertainty and a lack of a legal framework. Whether through the SEC's "Crypto Projects" or the Clarity Act, the US is expected to achieve long-awaited regulatory certainty as early as January. By the end of this year, we will no longer be discussing equity vs. tokens. We will be discussing ownership—transparent, transferable, legally protected, and natively digital ownership.

data.title: Predicting the Market 2025: Hype, Risks, and Regulatory Maneuvering
data.description:
Author: D50 Source: X, @Kristintt

In 2025, prediction markets were simultaneously attracting massive amounts of capital and frequently encountering problems, pushed to an unprecedented crossroads amidst the tug-of-war between hype, risk, and regulation.

Foreword:

If you only started paying attention to prediction markets in 2026, you'll likely have this feeling: prediction markets are so big, changing so fast, and so chaotic. In the two weeks since I started writing this article, the market has seen a series of events, including controversial transactions and reversals, platforms starting to charge transaction fees, old projects issuing new tokens, and new regulatory bills being debated again—I've found that there's no way to cover them all.

These changes didn't just happen in 2026; they're more like the result of continuous accumulation throughout 2025, exploding in the new year. In 2025, the prediction market experienced a true period of expansion. However, at the same time, the pressure on infrastructure, the blurring of regulatory boundaries, and the divergence in regulatory attitudes remained largely unaddressed. Therefore, this article won't chase after the new stories that have already unfolded in 2026, but instead chooses to return to 2025 and re-examine how the prediction market has arrived at its current state in just one year. Understanding 2025 might help you understand why the prediction market "has become what it is" in 2026.

Table of Contents

  • Market Overview

  • Main Projects

  • Main Mechanisms of Prediction Markets

  • User Gameplay

  • Ecosystem Building

  • Risks, Regulation, and Black Swans

The main text begins below. This article contains approximately 8.6k words (reading time approximately 30 minutes)

Figure - Forecast market (nominal) trading volume in 2025

In 2025, Wall Street capital entered Polymarket, and Kalshi also achieved rapid growth by leveraging its compliance advantages and multiple collaborations. Although the overall market still exhibits a duopoly, prediction market projects in other ecosystems and sectors have also emerged and achieved remarkable results in their respective sub-sectors.

...

Figure - 2025 Prediction Market Trading Volume Share

As the political prediction frenzy triggered by last year's US election gradually subsided, the sports market experienced a boom in 2025, propelling Kalshi to successfully reverse its previous market position. The changes in the two leading prediction market platforms show that 2025 became a crucial year for sports and political betting to dominate the market rhythm.

...

Figure - Polymarket and Kalshi Open Market Types

2. Major Projects

Entering 2025, in addition to significant changes in the original two major prediction markets, prediction market projects in other sub-sectors have also emerged in different forms. The following is a brief overview of the major prediction market projects that have launched their mainnets in 2025.

2.1 Polymarket: A Wall Street Newcomer, Still the Leader in On-Chain Prediction Markets

Polymarket is currently the undisputed leader in on-chain prediction markets. The platform primarily predicts the outcomes of various events. It first launched its mainnet on Polygon in 2020 and now supports more public chains and mainstream tokens for trading. Polymarket secured $150 million in funding in early 2025, but its most notable achievement was the $2 billion investment from ICE, the parent company of the NYSE, in October. This $2 billion check directly boosted Polymarket's valuation from $1.2 billion to $9 billion. From then on, Polymarket leaped from the gray market to a Wall Street newcomer, igniting the ecosystem's momentum in the prediction market in the second half of the year.

Following the high level of attention brought by the 2024 US presidential election, compliance pressures and market coverage have led Polymarket to shift its focus towards sports and other broader thematic markets. It has also partnered with Onyx Odds, the fastest-growing social sports prediction platform in the US, and UFC, the world's leading combat sports brand, to further implement this strategic plan. Currently, Polymarket's platform offers a diverse range of markets, but the largest share of trading volume remains largely related to political elections, followed by sports and cryptocurrencies. On the regulatory front, after acquiring the regulated exchange GCX and obtaining a license, Polymarket received good news at the end of 2025 – the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) finally ended its four-year regulatory restrictions, allowing it to return to the US market. Furthermore, Polymarket is also an official prediction market partner of X. Various investments and partnerships added a significant advantage to Polymarket in 2025, further solidifying its leading position in the on-chain prediction market. 2.2 Kalshi: A Magnificent Comeback Through Regulation and the Sports Market While Kalshi and Polymarket offer similar products, they follow different paths. Firstly, Kalshi's user base is limited to US users, and its payment methods are more traditional (e.g., debit cards, wire transfers). It also supports crypto payments, but its asset selection is far less diverse than Polymarket's. However, as an event-based prediction market that is not on-chain but has passed CFTC compliance regulations, Kalshi also possesses unique advantages. With Polymarket dominating the prediction market, Kalshi leveraged this advantage to stage a remarkable comeback: before 2025, Kalshi was far inferior to Polymarket in terms of both funding and transaction data. However, in 2025, it secured approximately $1.5 billion in funding through three rounds from established US VCs such as Paradigm, a16z, and Sequoia Capital, achieving a valuation of $11 billion, making it the highest-valued prediction market at the time. Another turning point occurred in March 2025. Like Polymarket, Kalshi recognized the enormous market potential of sports. It seized the opportunity presented by the March Madness tournament, the "elimination playoffs" of college basketball. Held annually in March, March Madness is one of the most popular annual events in the US, known for its single-elimination format, frequent upsets, and widespread betting. With its regulatory advantages and partnership with Robinhood, Kalshi amassed the largest betting community for March Madness. In March 2025, Kalshi achieved a fourfold increase in trading volume compared to the previous month. Following the "Crazy March," Kalshi reaped the benefits of the sports market, and through its continued partnership with Robinhood, a mainstream investment app for US retail investors, its trading momentum surged. In September of the same year, the NFL season began, and Kalshi attracted $441 million in trading volume during the opening week, continuously breaking daily trading volume records. This marked the first time Kalshi surpassed Polymarket in monthly trading volume, a significant turning point. For the next three months, Kalshi's platform trading volume consistently outpaced Polymarket, achieving a double harvest in both funding volume and platform trading volume in 2025.

Graph - Kalshi Transaction Volume and Tag Type Distribution

2.3 Limitless: A Short-Term High-Frequency Trading Player that Issues Tokens First

Besides Polymarket and Kalshi, the two leading prediction markets, Limitless, as the third largest, also achieved considerable results in 2025.

Chart - Limitless Monthly Trading Trends

2.4 Myriad: A Natural Flow Track Embedded in Media and Wallets

As the first native prediction market product built into a wallet in partnership with Trustwallet, it currently supports the Abstract, Linea, and BNB ecosystems.

Chart - Myriad Monthly Transaction Volume Trend

2.5 Football.Fun: Rekindling the Dawn of Gamefi in Prediction Markets

Football.Fun also takes a different approach, combining sports-related Gamefi with prediction markets based on the Base chain.

Football.Fun also takes a different approach, combining sports-related Gamefi with prediction markets based on the Base chain.

The platform's main gameplay involves obtaining virtual players through a gacha system. These virtual players then form teams and automatically participate in matches, with players profiting from the final results by holding onto these players. This gameplay is relatively rare in Chinese-speaking regions but is very popular in the US market. In the early days of sports betting in the US, it was a highly popular gray area industry, known as "Fantasy Sports." The platform currently offers free and professional versions, both with identical gameplay mechanics, but the former uses platform points while the latter uses real money. In July 2025, Football.Fun secured $2 million in funding from VCs such as 6th Man Ventures and Sfermion, and quickly completed a token sale exceeding $10 million in December, but did not achieve a TGE (Trusted Token Expiration) in 2025 (Note: It was TGEed on January 15, 2026). Compared to the lack of mention in the Chinese-speaking world, Football.Fun has gained significant attention in the European and American markets. When it was launched in early August, its actual monthly trading volume once surged to nearly $40 million, briefly surpassing Limitless. However, its popularity subsequently declined, and it has remained in a lukewarm state.

Chart - Football.Fun Monthly (Nominal) Trading Volume Trend

2.6 Opinion: Firing the First Shot in the BSC Prediction Market

Opinion, as the first BNB ecosystem prediction market project incubated by YZi Labs in 2024, leverages 2025 Binance's focus on prediction markets in 2016 not only garnered significant attention in the Chinese-speaking world but also led to a successful $5 million funding round from institutions such as YZi Labs, Amber Group, and echi. At the end of the year, Binance announced the completion of a new round of funding worth tens of millions of dollars. While both Opinion and Polymarket are event-driven prediction markets, Opinion employs a more crypto-native trading incentive approach. After launching its mainnet in October, Opinion initially used an invitation code system before opening full access to the public, simultaneously introducing rebates and points programs. This enabled Opinion to achieve a remarkable monthly trading volume exceeding 100 million in its first month, and it has continued to break new records since.

Chart - Opinion Monthly Trading Volume Trend

2.7 Predict.fun: A Profitable Prediction Market Embraced by Binance

Predict.fun is a prediction market that uses "predicting funds while generating returns" as its selling point. It was already established in September 2024.

Layer 2 Blast, focusing on native yields, launched its mainnet. The project's founder is a former Binance executive who, despite leaving due to negative controversies, showed signs of improvement in his relationship with CZ in 2025. In December of the same year, CZ announced the official launch of Predct.fun on the BNB Chain, making it another prediction market project in the BNB ecosystem. Predct.fun also adopted a points system after its launch, achieving over $70 million in initial trading volume in December. 3. Key Mechanisms of Prediction Markets 3.1 Oracles Although most prediction markets are currently event-driven, the oracles used vary.

Figure - Oracle Mechanisms Used in Mainstream Markets

As a true on-chain prediction market, unlike Kalshi's centralized team auditing approach, Polymarket primarily uses the UMA Optimistic Oracle and upgraded from OOv2 to MOOv2 in August 2025. In September, it introduced Chainlink as its primary data source.

The basic mechanism of UMA is as follows: an oracle bot submits an answer, which is initially considered correct. If anyone questions it, arbitration is conducted; otherwise, settlement takes place. The MOOv2 version adds a customization and management module compared to the OOv2 version, supporting the setting of parameters such as permissions and order book deposits. In the settlement process, DVM is UMA's decentralized adjudication system. UMA token holders can participate in voting and influence the final result (which has thus become one of the methods of malicious prediction market manipulation, as will be discussed later). The entire settlement process of UMA can be simplified as shown in the following diagram:

Diagram - Optimistic Oracle Settlement and DVM Arbitration Process

Another oracle used by Polymarket, Chainlink—as the head oracle—primarily uses multiple nodes to verify and transmit data. Each node obtains information from different data sources, and finally aggregates this data to arrive at the final result. Currently, it is mainly used to improve the efficiency of Polymarket's data platform services.

3.2 Order Book

Most prediction markets currently use the CLOB (Central Limit Order Book) trading model, which is a trading mechanism that centralizes all buy and sell orders in a single order book for matching and execution. This lays the foundation for order execution in prediction markets: in the central limit order book, traders can choose to place limit orders or market orders, and the system will match these buy and sell orders according to price and time priority.

To reduce latency while ensuring capital utilization, most prediction markets adopt an off-chain matching and on-chain settlement approach. Opinion is somewhat unique; based on its trust in the high performance of the BNB Chain, its orders are executed on-chain from matching to settlement, using a fully on-chain central limit order book.

Predict.fun uses a single order book model – specifically designed for binary prediction markets, displaying only buy and sell orders for "YES" while calculating the order book for "NO" in real time through mathematical complementarity. However, it also borrows from a central limit order book approach, using a unified matching logic. The overall order book is also managed through on-chain settlement and off-chain maintenance and matching. Meanwhile, a small number of prediction markets still use AMMs (Automated Market Makers). Initially, Polymarket used a similar AMM-like LMSR algorithm, but switched to a decentralized order book in 2025. This is because AMMs, widely used in DeFi, are not actually well-suited for prediction markets due to market structural issues.

Figure - Order Book Types Used in Mainstream Markets

4. User Play Methods

Currently, prediction markets are still in the exploratory stage, and various play methods are emerging. Here, we will take a landmark platform as an example and simply divide the current common play methods into three categories: directional trading, market-neutral strategies, and market maker profit strategies.

4.2 Market Neutral Strategy

The market neutral strategy breaks through the cognitive limitations of directional trading. Users do not need to understand the market background, but instead use various platform structures or time differences to profit from pricing deviations. (1) Sweeping the End of the Day As a high-probability trading strategy, sweeping the end of the day allows users to use a large amount of funds to buy when the market has high certainty—such as when the probability of an event is as high as 90+%. However, even with high certainty, a sudden market downturn may lead to serious losses of funds. (2) Structural Arbitrage Profits are made by using the structural problems of platforms and markets. This arbitrage method usually requires calculation using mathematical formulas. These arbitrage methods all use the same mathematical formula (the sum of the purchase costs of all outcomes is less than $1): Outcome(1) + Outcome(2) + ... + Outcome(n) < $1. The outcomes here must first satisfy a necessary condition: they are mutually exclusive and exhaustive (they cannot occur simultaneously, and one outcome must occur). Since you hold a position regardless of which outcome is ultimately determined, your final profit equals $1 minus the cost. The above inequality occurs in the following situations: a. Cross-platform arbitrage: The market for the same event may exist on different platforms, and the judgment results of these markets are usually consistent. Therefore, users can selectively purchase all results on different platforms to profit, provided that the sum of the purchase costs of each result is less than $1.

b. Cross-condition arbitrage: The same event may have multiple markets on the same platform, but the descriptions are not exactly the same. It can also be purchased at a price where the sum of the costs of all results is less than $1. c. Multi-result arbitrage: Commonly seen in election markets, the result YES usually has many options. If the sum of the purchase costs of all YES results is less than $1, it can also be purchased.

d. Single-result arbitrage: Commonly seen in price prediction markets, usually involving YES/NO predictions for the same price. If the cost of purchasing both YES and NO simultaneously is less than $1, a limit order can be used. (3) Triad market arbitrage: The triad market here mostly refers to the sports market, where there are only three possible outcomes: A wins, B wins, and a draw. Based on these three results, the price should conform to the following equation: A wins + draw = B wins. Therefore, when the market price shows such an inequality: A wins + draw < B wins, it can be concluded that the price of "B wins" is undervalued, and a buy order should be placed. (4) Price spread arbitrage The factors that cause price spreads are generally price imbalances caused by market making delays, hence it is also called "market making price spread imbalance arbitrage". This situation is often caused by problems such as poor liquidity, asymmetry between buy and sell orders, emotional rush to buy, and market maker price delays. The operation process is usually as follows: a. Find markets with large bid-ask spreads b. Use limit orders to buy at low prices c. Use limit orders to sell at high prices However, this arbitrage method requires users to have a high sense of market fluctuations and has a certain threshold. (5) Development of trading robots Trading robots based on prediction markets are currently a very popular direction in prediction markets. In the prediction market, which has high requirements for speed and information, the addition of robots is undoubtedly a great help to players a. Arbitrage robots As mentioned above, this arbitrage method composed of mathematical formulas is very suitable for robots to execute. Therefore, there are various arbitrage robots with different functions on the market, including binary market arbitrage robots, three-party market arbitrage robots, etc. b. Trend-following trading bots. These bots typically act opportunistically, providing liquidity to those experiencing price increases (actively placing sell orders to users chasing the rise) and buying at lower prices during periods of panic, waiting to sell after the next upward surge. c. LLM-based trading bots. When users lack market knowledge or sufficient information, LLM-based trading bots typically use data analysis to assist in making trading decisions. d. Copy trading bots. These bots track traders with high prediction success rates in the market and follow their trades. The selection criteria for specific traders are generally based on their equity curve, preferred markets, and profit/loss areas. e. Preemptive Robots The spread of information takes time. During the period from the occurrence of an event and its promotion to the start of order book submissions, robots can use automated processes to preempt ordinary manual trading users. Of course, these robots are also more susceptible to interference from fake news. 4.3 Market Maker Revenue Strategies As mentioned earlier, most prediction markets use a central limit order book trading model. Compared to AMMs, this trading model shifts market makers from a passive risk-bearing position to an active one. Except for platforms like Kalshi that cooperate with professional market maker institutions, the market maker role in most prediction markets is actively undertaken by users. Therefore, prediction markets also provide additional rewards to market makers who provide liquidity to the market through limit orders. Prediction markets like Polymarket distribute approximately $500,000 in trading volume per month. Becoming a market maker to earn profits also requires certain qualifications. First, users need to review the market's reward rules for market makers and filter the market accordingly. The market typically stipulates the minimum position size and the maximum allowed bid-ask spread for market makers to receive rewards. For market makers, the closer their limit orders are to the midpoint, the more generous the rewards will be. This reward mechanism exhibits a quadratic function characteristic—the further away from the midpoint, the faster the rewards diminish. Simultaneously, the system removes certain edge orders used to create "false midpoints" to prevent manipulation. Furthermore, market makers must consider the balance between risk and reward. The longer a market maker places an order, the more rewards accumulate, but longer periods also mean the potential for losses due to extreme market conditions. Discussions about arbitrage methods are raging in the market, from user-developed scripts to the emergence of professional arbitrage platforms and the commercial sale of arbitrage services. Participants are all trying to get a share of this seemingly complex game. However, due to Polymarket's use of an off-chain order book, neutral arbitrage is gradually becoming more of a tool. Given the limited order book depth in most markets, arbitrage competition is shifting from "discovering price differences" to "competing on execution speed." Simultaneously, market makers must constantly weigh their ultimate gains against platform subsidies and passive losses from placing orders. Under this structure, the arbitrage opportunities left for ordinary users are limited, and ultimately, only players with both rule understanding and risk management capabilities can truly benefit.

5. Ecosystem Building

As a platform centered on trading activity, the growth of prediction markets depends on continuously creating new trading demands and use cases. As a relatively mature representative of the ecosystem, Polymarket launched a Builder Program in 2025. This program encourages developers to build products around Polymarket, guiding more users to participate in trading, thereby driving the overall liquidity and trading volume of the platform, and providing corresponding incentives and support for outstanding builders.

Figure - Polymarket Builders Program Equity Table

Currently, Polymarket Builders projects are mainly divided into two categories: Trading Terminal and TG Bot.

Figure - Partial list of Polymarket Builders projects (ranked by trading volume)

These products mainly attempt to solve the current user experience and trading strategy problems of the Polymarket platform:

1. For users takers, Polymarket has a trading latency of approximately 500ms. This is an intentional anti-preemptive measure set by the platform. Additionally, the platform's servers located in Europe and London also experience some network latency.

In this situation, platform front-end still frequently experiences lag and data refresh delays. Therefore, most Trading Terminals and TG Bots optimize the user's order placement experience as a taker by leveraging official APIs and WebSocket RTDS real-time data sources, and simplifying order value settings. 2. In predictive markets, information advantage is crucial for directional trading. Therefore, many Trading Terminals use AI to capture real-time news related to market conditions, and users can use AI to access relevant materials or probability analysis to help them better understand market conditions and even make trades based on the information obtained. 3. Insider addresses, whales, and smart money are often highly watched trading entities in the market, and many products have also launched address tracking and copy trading functions. 4. In addition, there are some niche functions, such as spread detection for arbitrage users, predictive market ETFs, and multiple AI-analyzed market conditions. Through the analysis of these Polymarket builders' products, it's clear that prediction markets still face many user experience issues. However, this approach of incentivizing users to optimize their products not only provides existing users with a more comfortable trading experience but also attracts more new users with lower barriers to entry. The market's positive flywheel is leading more users into the ecosystem. 6. Risks, Regulation, and Black Swans 2025 was not a year of smooth development for prediction markets. Systemic shocks and unexpected events occurred one after another, such as platform outages, insider trading issues, and regulatory frictions, which continued to impact the market. Polymarket, as a leader, experienced multiple outages and service interruptions over its 25 years. Besides Polygon's underlying chain performance issues, subgraph data and the API search engine also frequently malfunctioned. Official data shows that Polymarket experienced frequent issues across its services in November. Server failures often resulted in losses for arbitrage users, and its infrastructure has long been criticized by the market. Furthermore, Polymarket also experienced a security incident in 2025 where a vulnerability in its external authentication provider allowed attackers to gain unauthorized access to some platform accounts; fortunately, only a small number of users were affected.

Chart - Polymarket November (2025) System Performance

Markets need to "create gods" to attract users, and prediction markets also provide retail investors with many tempting cases of making big profits with small investments. ... However, market data reveals a harsh reality: 70% of traders are losing money, while the top 0.04% of the remaining 30% who profit capture more than half of the 70% of profits. This concentration of profits at the top is approaching that of the perpetual contract market. The rampant insider trading and event manipulation are among the reasons for this concentration of profits. In October 2025, 12 hours before the Nobel Prize announcement, a newly registered user made a large, precise bet on the Peace Prize winner; shortly afterward, another trader heavily bet on an unpopular option in the Google search rankings and ultimately profited. Only afterward did ordinary players realize that the search rankings were generated based on Google's internal algorithm, and any slight adjustment to any parameter could change the results, completely invisible to external users. Compared to these insider trading practices, some manipulation tactics are even more abhorrent to ordinary users: Polymarket's DVM arbitration mechanism provides opportunities for malicious actors. As mentioned earlier, DVM arbitration is primarily conducted by UMA token holders, allowing UMA whales to use token voting to force decisions that contradict the truth. Although the official stance was ultimately that the decision was unfair, no compensation was offered to those who suffered losses. Insider trading and manipulation are not new in betting games, and various manipulation scandals are commonplace in the traditional gambling industry. However, crypto prediction markets conveniently provide these insiders/manipulators with a channel to conceal the source and destination of their funds. It is precisely for these reasons that the conflict between prediction markets and regulators is constant.

Figure - Prediction Markets and US Regulation in 2025

Prediction markets like Kalshi, which are already regulated by the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), will still experience significant friction with US regulators in 2025.

At the federal level, the CFTC classifies prediction markets as derivatives contracts/futures, allowing them to operate as long as they remain within the regulatory framework. However, at the state level, different states have their own regulatory agencies and laws. This two-tiered regulatory structure makes the US one of the most complex and prone to regulatory friction globally. Regulatory conflicts arise when some states claim that state laws take precedence over federal exemptions. Sports prediction markets are easily categorized as "gambling" by state regulators, and Kalshi's market is predominantly comprised of sports prediction markets, leading many states and agencies to file cease and desist orders against Kalshi. However, there is some good news on the regulatory front. Polymarket, after being accused by the CFTC of violating US futures laws in 2022, successfully returned to the US market in November 2025 through a shell company.

Figure - Global Regulatory Attitudes Towards Prediction Markets in 2025

Looking globally, European and Asian countries consistently hold varying degrees of prohibition towards prediction markets. Although prediction markets seem to be gradually being accepted by mainstream finance in 2025, their real challenge remains the insurmountable mountain of regulation.

data.title: Crypto and AI need female executives
data.description:
Author: Alice Deep Tide TechFlow

Recently, I've noticed an interesting phenomenon in the AI ​​industry: more and more female executives are stepping into the limelight.

On December 30th, Meta announced its acquisition of Manus for a hefty $2 billion. CZ Chen, the 90s-born COO, has entered the public eye. She holds a bachelor's degree from Shanghai University of Finance and Economics and a master's degree from Columbia University. She started working in 2018, successively holding positions at Vanke and financial advisory firms. In 2024, she made her final leap to Manus, achieving financial freedom immediately.

On January 9th, at the MiniMax IPO bell-ringing ceremony, standing on the stage alongside 36-year-old founder Yan Junjie was a woman born in 1994, Yun Yeyi. This 31-year-old COO is now worth HK$4.8 billion. What is Yun Yeyi's background? Yan Junjie graduated from Johns Hopkins University with a degree in Electrical Engineering and minored in Economics and Mathematics. After graduating in 2017, she joined SenseTime, rising from a financing manager to assistant to CEO Xu Li, and then to director of the Innovation Business Department, witnessing SenseTime's journey from a unicorn to a Hong Kong stock exchange listing. In 2022, Yan Junjie decided to leave SenseTime to found MiniMax, and Yun Yeyi followed him almost without hesitation. Her value goes beyond simply following. MiniMax's prospectus shows that Yun Yeyi handles almost all aspects of the company except for technology research and development: product, commercialization, board of directors, operations, management... Her annual salary is $1.479 million, more than all the other executive directors combined, which speaks volumes. Not only in China, but globally in the AI ​​field, the power of women is undeniable. Daniela Amodei, with a background in English literature, after working at Stripe and OpenAI, co-founded Anthropic with her brother Dario in 2021, serving as president, focusing on daily operations and commercialization, and driving the marketization of Claude products. Lila Ibrahim, a former Intel executive, joined DeepMind in 2018 as its first COO, responsible for day-to-day operations, partnerships, social impact, external affairs, and government relations. Mira Murati, an Albanian former CTO of OpenAI, came to the US on a scholarship at age 16, worked on the Tesla Model X team, then moved to OpenAI, eventually leaving to found Thinking Machines Lab, valued at $9 billion… This scenario seems familiar. From 2017 to 2021, the golden age of crypto saw a constellation of stars, and one of the most striking features was the presence of female CMOs and COOs. The most well-known is Binance co-founder and CMO He Yi (now co-CEO). From Shanghai to Tokyo, from Malta to Paris and then to Dubai, she was present at every strategic relocation, helping the company become the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange. Lisa Loud, from Apple engineer to PayPal's head of Canadian markets, joined BitMEX as CMO in 2017, after which BitMEX became the world's largest crypto derivatives trading platform. Cynthia Wu, COO of Matrixport and former Vice President of Product Development at the Hong Kong Stock Exchange, brought her experience in traditional finance to crypto financial services, helping the company become one of Asia's largest digital asset service platforms. Once upon a time, crypto was the focus of global assets, and the spotlight naturally shone on these female executives standing at the center of the stage. But the tide has receded, and the protagonists have changed. Now, AI is the focus of the spotlight, and we've seen Daniela Amodei on the Forbes rich list and Yun Yeyi triumphantly ringing the bell at MiniMax's IPO. In essence, crypto and AI share a striking similarity—both cutting-edge and seemingly outdated. The cutting-edge nature of technology is reflected in the technology itself. Blockchain restructures trust mechanisms, and AI restructures productivity; these are fundamental technologies capable of changing the world. The "rustic" aspect is reflected in the founders' profiles. Most have technical backgrounds and are intimately familiar with code, but are unfamiliar with marketing, especially government relations and public relations. This is where the value of female COOs/CMOs lies. They act as a bridge between technical geniuses and the outside world, capable of deep dialogue with the technical team and telling compelling stories to investors and users. Daniela Amodei transformed her AI security philosophy into an actionable business strategy, enabling Claude to break through the shadow of ChatGPT; Yun Yeyi took MiniMax from the laboratory to the consumer market; He Yi served as chief customer officer for a long time, personally answering users' questions and building trust. When a product moves beyond the purely technical stage and needs to be more consumer-facing, the advantages of female executives become more apparent. After all, public relations and product development require empathy, not confrontational thinking. From another perspective, capable female executives will vote with their feet; they will go where they can showcase their talents and create value. If they start leaving an industry, it indicates that the industry's commercial certainty is disappearing. The current problem in the crypto industry is obvious: a lack of talent capable of translating technology into products that are widely accepted. Mass adoption and positive externalities remain mere rhetoric. This pattern is evident in any emerging industry: an industry truly shifts from technology-driven to commercial and mass-market-oriented when female executives with technical understanding, business acumen, and strong narrative skills begin to rise. Their emergence signifies the true maturity of the industry. The AI ​​sector has already experienced this turning point, with female executives like Daniela Amodei and Yun Yeyi driving the productization of technology, bringing AI from laboratory algorithms into everyday life and the business world. The crypto industry, however, deserves to remain mired in ruts of PvP if it cannot retain "elites who can speak like human beings." The flow of talent is a barometer of industry trends. Wherever they go, value is created; where they leave, the bubble often bursts.

data.title: Hong Kong’s Stablecoin Licensing Framework Set to Begin Early 2026, Financial Secretary Paul Chan Confirms
data.description:
Hong Kong Sets Sights On Stablecoin Hub Status

Hong Kong’s financial leaders are making no secret of their ambition to lead the global digital finance race.

During the World Economic Forum in Davos on 20 January 2026, Financial Secretary Paul Chan confirmed that the city is preparing to issue its first batch of stablecoin licences within the first quarter of this year.

🇭🇰 HONG KONG TO ISSUE FIRST STABLECOIN LICENSES IN Q1!

Hong Kong’s financial chief says the city will grant its first official stablecoin licenses in Q1 2026.

It comes after HK rolled out new stablecoin rules last year requiring issuers to meet strict reserve, redemption and… pic.twitter.com/wg8LTVLmr5

— Coin Bureau (@coinbureau) January 21, 2026

This move is designed to anchor a comprehensive ecosystem that bridges the gap between traditional capital markets and the burgeoning world of digital assets.

Addressing a gathering hosted by Animoca Brands, Chan explained that the government’s strategy is built on a "proactive yet prudent approach" to innovation.

He noted that by applying the principle of "same activity, same risk, same regulation," Hong Kong intends to foster a market that is both responsible and sustainable.

The Financial Secretary explained that financial innovations like digital assets help to improve transparency and efficiency while helping to direct capital toward the real economy.

He also pointed to how blockchain applications are already transforming capital markets through developments such as asset tokenisation and genomic data analytics.

Hong Kong’s digital asset vision on the global stage. Financial Secretary Paul Chan continued his engagements at the World Economic Forum Annual Meeting in Davos yesterday (Jan 20), joining Vice Premier of the State Council He Lifeng’s Special Address and connecting with senior… pic.twitter.com/Vz2MSMILF6

— BrandHongKong 香港亞洲國際都會 (@Brand_HK) January 21, 2026

Will Stablecoin Rules Bring Market Stability?

The upcoming licensing regime, which follows legislation passed in 2025, sets a high bar for any firm wishing to issue fiat-referenced tokens.

To qualify, issuers must prove they hold 100% reserves in high-quality, liquid assets and guarantee that users can redeem their tokens at par value within one business day.

These strict standards on reserves and risk management arrive at a time when the global stablecoin market has swelled beyond $310 billion.

This framework is the latest addition to a regulatory structure that has already brought 11 virtual asset trading platforms under official oversight since 2023.

Major players such as OSL, HashKey, and Bullish are among those already operating under the watchful eye of the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC).

By bringing stablecoins into the fold, the city aims to create a "closed-loop" system of regulated intermediaries that protects retail investors from the volatility often seen in offshore markets.

Can Tokenisation Drive Real Economic Growth?

While much of the public focus remains on trading, the Hong Kong government is proving the technology's worth through its own balance sheet.

The city has now issued three separate batches of tokenised green bonds, totalling approximately $2.1 billion.

One recent issuance saw a significant $1.3 billion offering that attracted more than $16 billion in orders from global investors.

Furthering this push, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) recently transitioned Project Ensemble into a new pilot phase.

This initiative allows major banks and asset managers to test real-value transactions using tokenised deposits.

🇭🇰Hong Kong pushes tokenisation forward

The HKMA has launched EnsembleTX, moving Project

Ensemble from experiments to real-value transactions using tokenised deposits & digital assets. 👇

- Built on the 2024 Ensemble Sandbox
- Enables faster, more transparent settlement
-… pic.twitter.com/i1L5QBGkz6

— Cyprx Research Lab Official (@CyprxResearch) November 26, 2025

Unlike previous experiments, this phase focuses on interbank settlements and 24/7 liquidity management, effectively treating digital ledger technology as the future plumbing of the city’s financial infrastructure.

Are High Compliance Costs Deterring Investors?

Despite the official optimism, not everyone is convinced that more regulation is always better.

The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Professionals Association has raised concerns that the tightening of rules for virtual asset management could backfire.

They warned on Tuesday that the high costs associated with stricter licensing thresholds might deter traditional asset managers from entering the space, potentially slowing down institutional adoption just as the city seeks to differentiate itself from regional competitors.

🇭🇰Hong Kong Group Warns on Crypto Fund Rules

Hong Kong’s HKSFPA criticized proposed rules that would require even a 1% crypto allocation to get a full virtual asset license, calling it disproportionate and costly, reported by @TheBlock__.

The group also opposed custody rules… pic.twitter.com/7iugxsedCv

— ME Group (@MetaEraHK) January 20, 2026

How Does Hong Kong Balance Innovation With Global Ties?

Beyond the digital asset circuit, Paul Chan utilised his time in Davos to reinforce Hong Kong's identity as a bastion of free trade.

He held discussions with World Trade Organization Director-General Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala to reiterate support for multilateralism.

Chan also conducted bilateral meetings with representatives from Switzerland, Thailand, Egypt, and various Gulf states, alongside high-profile technology investors like Robert Smith of Vista Equity Partners.

As Hong Kong continues to refine its "regulatory sandbox" for innovation, it remains in the delicate position of building a world-class crypto hub while navigating the broader restrictions on cryptocurrency activity within mainland China.

The first quarter of 2026 will be a defining period as the first licensed stablecoin issuers are named, marking a new chapter in the city's financial evolution.

data.title: Are Gold and Silver Becoming the Safer Bet While Bitcoin Faces Uncertainty?
data.description:
When US President Donald Trump announced his intent to finalise the "complete and total purchase of Greenland," the global financial hierarchy underwent a sudden and dramatic shift.

As of 21 January 2026, a clear divide has emerged in the markets.

While ancient metals are scaling vertical peaks, the "digital gold" narrative is facing a harsh reality check.

The current geopolitical climate has forced a separation between traditional safe havens and their modern counterparts.

Why Are Gold And Silver Hitting Record Highs?

The rush for physical safety has pushed precious metals into uncharted territory.

International gold prices (XAU/USD) climbed to over $4,800 per ounce, having breached the $4,700 milestone for the first time only a day prior.

BREAKING: Gold extends gains to a record $4,850/oz, now up +$260 in 48 hours.

We are all witnessing history right now. pic.twitter.com/wMkRFkEqrU

— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) January 21, 2026

Silver has been even more aggressive; the metal recently touched $94.87, marking its fifth record high in 2026 alone.

#Silver

Silver has maintained a strong upward trend. It has currently reached a new high of $94.1. 📈📈

Looking ahead, silver prices are expected to continue rising due to industrial and AI sector demand.

Strong buying liquidity will continue to push silver prices above $100. pic.twitter.com/kVsW8crgRn

— Shirley (@YShirley_XAUUSD) January 19, 2026

The catalyst is a sudden escalation in trade hostility following President Trump’s plan to impose a 10% tariff on eight European nations, including the UK, France, and Germany, set to begin on 1 February 2026.

These levies could jump to 25% by June if a deal for Greenland is not reached.

BREAKING: Trump announces he will impose a 10% tariff on 8 European countries starting February 1st, which will then increase to 25% starting June 1st until deal is reached for the “complete and total purchase of Greenland”. pic.twitter.com/eFriBltPX1

— Benny Johnson (@bennyjohnson) January 17, 2026

Sim Subin, a researcher at Kiwoom Securities, noted that the current rally is driven by a desperate search for assets with "no issuer risk" as fiscal deficits and geopolitical threats mount.

Is Bitcoin Losing Its Safe Haven Status?

While gold and silver continue to climb, Bitcoin has struggled to hold its ground, dipping to $87,829 earlier in the morning and now recovering slightly to $89,224.

JUST IN: Bitcoin falls under $88,000 pic.twitter.com/M6yCuDK12m

— Watcher.Guru (@WatcherGuru) January 20, 2026

This follows a brief peak of $96,000 earlier this year.

The drop mirrors a broader retreat in risk-on assets, with Dow Jones futures falling 378 points and the Nasdaq 100 dropping 1.1% in response to the tariff news.

Bloomberg analyst Eric Balchunas suggests this is merely a cyclical correction.

Addressing the divergence on X, he wrote:

"Bitcoin was up 122% in 2024, $GLD and $SLV were up 20%. It got ahead of itself, needed breather, other assets just playing catch up."

Don't worry I got you. Bitcoin was up 122% in 2024, $GLD and $SLV were up 20%. It got ahead of itself, needed breather, other assets just playing catch up. Feel better?

— Eric Balchunas (@EricBalchunas) January 20, 2026

Despite the optimistic "breather" outlook, Bitcoin’s recent behaviour has aligned more closely with high-beta equities than with gold.

During market uptrends over the past year, it often lagged behind the Nasdaq, yet it fully participated in the subsequent downturns.

Will The Great Decoupling Continue?

For years, Bitcoin, gold, and silver moved in tandem as alternatives to the US dollar.

However, a decoupling is now undeniable.

Last year, gold rose 64% and silver surged 142%, while Bitcoin actually dipped 6%.

This gap persists because, as geopolitical risks heighten, institutional investors are returning to the centuries-old reliability of metals over the volatility of code.

However, some market experts see an opportunity in this split.

Analyst PlanB has proposed that a diversified allocation, specifically 80% gold and 20% Bitcoin, can maximise returns while keeping risk profiles manageable.

Gold and bitcoin investors love to argue about who is smarter. But gold and bitcoin are in the same team and risk/return profile (calmar) is almost identical. Best investment is a combi of gold AND bitcoin. E.g. 80% gold + 20% bitcoin has LESS risk and 2x MORE return than gold. pic.twitter.com/Cs3PFPA25o

— PlanB (@100trillionUSD) January 20, 2026

Others predict that once the initial surge in metals cools, investors may rotate their profits back into the "digital gold" of the crypto markets.

How Is The Rest Of The World Reacting?

The tension has sent tremors far beyond Washington and Brussels.

In Japan, bond markets are in a state of historic flux.

The yield on 40-year government bonds climbed past 4%, a level not seen since the security was introduced.

Shorter-term 10-year yields reached 2.3%, their highest since 1999.

As European leaders hold emergency meetings to counter the looming February tariffs, the focus remains on whether Bitcoin can eventually reclaim its status as a hedge.

For now, the "Greenland premium" belongs exclusively to gold and silver.

They are openly threatening to conquer other countries now to strip them of their precious minerals and natural resources.

Greenland has about 1.5 million tons of rare earth element reserves, ranking it in the top 10 globally — and Trump and his oligarch donors want it. pic.twitter.com/xF60vIaDiY

— Melanie D'Arrigo (@DarrigoMelanie) January 3, 2026

data.title: Gold Hits $4,739 and Silver Soars as Bitcoin Wobbles Amid Market Chaos
data.description:
Gold And Silver Both Hits Record High While Bitcoin Struggles

Gold surged to a fresh all-time high of $4,739 per ounce in futures trading as investors rushed into traditional safe-haven assets amid escalating geopolitical and economic uncertainty.

Gold not stopping, almost $5,000 per ounce. pic.twitter.com/FEaUCxtTfj

— Sajjad Mohmand (@SajjadTribal) January 21, 2026

In contrast, Bitcoin slipped to around $90,900, underscoring growing volatility in digital assets following U.S. President Donald Trump’s latest tariff threats against Europe.

BTC’s slip and rebound between ~$87K–$91K suggests support is still respected even under stress,

an early sign that buyers haven’t completely left. pic.twitter.com/6yuh6awDb0

— Tom 😾 (@TomWeb33) January 21, 2026

The rally in precious metals has extended well beyond gold. Silver climbed to $94.68 per ounce, notching five record highs in 2026 alone, as investors sought shelter from rising macroeconomic risk. Over the past 12 months, gold has surged more than 81%, marking one of its strongest annual performances on record and reinforcing its long-standing role as a hedge during periods of market stress.

Market anxiety intensified after Trump announced plans to impose 10% tariffs on imports from eight European countries, with the rate set to rise to 25% by June if negotiations fail. The proposed measures—linked to Trump’s controversial push for the “complete and total purchase of Greenland”—have reignited fears of a broader transatlantic trade conflict. European leaders reportedly convened emergency meetings as global markets reacted sharply.

Trump announces sweeping EU tariffs against Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, UK and others until Europe hands over Greenland to the US.

Left unsaid: This is about to be a huge tax on Americans who use prescription drugs from EU pharma companies. pic.twitter.com/som0ihz9QM

— Max Burns (@themaxburns) January 17, 2026

U.S. equity futures reflected the growing unease, with Dow Jones futures pointing to a 378-point decline, S&P 500 futures down 0.9%, and Nasdaq 100 futures sliding 1.1%, signaling a weak start to trading.

The shockwaves extended to bond markets, particularly in Japan, where the 40-year government bond yield climbed above 4%, its highest level since inception. Shorter-dated bonds also sold off, with the 10-year yield rising to 2.3%—levels not seen since 1999—and the 20-year yield jumping to 3.35%, highlighting widespread investor caution.

Ethereum, XRP And Solana All Suffers

Other cryptocurrencies weren't spared from the sell-off. Beyond Bitcoin’s pullback, Ethereum dropped more than 3% over 24 hours, while XRP and Solana declined over 2%, reflecting a broader retreat from risk assets as capital flowed into safer alternatives.

What price will Ethereum hit in 2026?

Do you often buy the dips like today?
It seems like a great opportunity to play this move.
It’s simple: I’m confident that ETH will reach 3,500 this year there are still 11+ months ahead, and the chart will swing back and forth many more… pic.twitter.com/9cuEOb3vUG

— Thearbi (@zhuravlioff) January 21, 2026

Still, some market observers see a potential silver lining for crypto. Bitcoin investor Lark Davis noted that gold’s momentum toward the $5,000 level, a key Fibonacci extension, could eventually trigger a rotation of capital back into digital assets once precious metals peak.

For now, however, the divergence is clear: gold and silver are once again leading as crisis hedges, while Bitcoin and the broader crypto market grapple with heightened volatility tied to real-world political and trade shocks.

data.title: When Ethereum pays interest to TradFi: Is ETH reaching a structural inflection point?
data.description:
Holding an Ethereum ETF now allows you to receive regular interest payments, much like holding bonds? Earlier this month, Grayscale announced that its Grayscale Ethereum Staking ETF (ETHE) had distributed staking rewards earned between October 6, 2025, and December 31, 2025, to existing shareholders. This marks the first time a U.S. spot crypto asset trading product has distributed staking rewards to its holders. While this may seem like a routine on-chain practice to Web3 Native users, in the context of crypto finance history, it signifies that Ethereum's native rewards have been packaged within the standard framework of traditional finance for the first time, undoubtedly a milestone. More importantly, this is not an isolated event. At the on-chain data level, the Ethereum staking rate continues to climb, validators leaving the queue are gradually being absorbed, and those entering the queue are accumulating again—a series of changes are happening simultaneously. These seemingly scattered signals are collectively pointing to a deeper question: Is Ethereum gradually evolving from an asset primarily driven by price fluctuations into an "interest-bearing asset" accepted by long-term capital and possessing stable return attributes?

I. ETF Profit Distribution: Traditional Investors' "First Experience" with Staking

Objectively speaking, for a long time, Ethereum staking was more like a technical experiment with a geeky vibe, limited to the "on-chain world."

Because it not only requires users to have basic cryptographic knowledge such as wallets and private keys, but also to understand the validator mechanism, consensus rules, lock-up period, and penalty logic. Although Liquidity Staking (LSD) protocols, represented by Lido Finance, have lowered the barrier to entry to a considerable extent, the staking rewards themselves still mainly remain in the native crypto context (stETH and other wrapped tokens).

Ultimately, for most Web2 investors, this system is neither intuitive nor easily accessible, representing a significant hurdle. Now, this hurdle is being bridged by ETFs. According to Grayscale's distribution plan, ETHE holders will receive $0.083178 for each share they hold. This amount reflects the proceeds from staking and selling of shares during the corresponding period, and the distribution will take place on January 6, 2026 (the distribution date), targeting investors who held ETHE shares as of January 5, 2026 (the record date). In short, the returns don't come from business operations, but from network security and consensus participation itself. Previously, such returns existed almost exclusively within the crypto industry, but now they're being packaged in the familiar financial shell of ETFs. Through US stock accounts, traditional 401(k) or mutual fund investors can obtain native returns (in USD) generated by Ethereum network consensus without accessing their private keys. It's important to emphasize that this doesn't mean Ethereum staking has achieved full compliance, nor does it mean regulators have given a unified interpretation of ETF staking services. However, in economic terms, a key change has occurred: **non-crypto native users, for the first time, indirectly obtain native returns generated by Ethereum network consensus without needing to understand nodes, private keys, or on-chain operations.** From this perspective, ETF returns are not an isolated event, but rather the first step for Ethereum staking to enter the broader capital market's view.

Grayscale is not an isolated case either. 21Shares' Ethereum ETF also announced that it will distribute the proceeds earned from staking ETH to existing shareholders. The distribution amount is $0.010378 per share, and the relevant ex-dividend and payment process has been disclosed simultaneously.

This is undoubtedly a great start, especially for institutions like Grayscale and 21Shares, which have influence in both TradFi and Web3. Its demonstration effect goes far beyond a single dividend payout; it will undoubtedly drive the effective and widespread adoption of Ethereum staking and yield distribution at the institutional level. It also signifies that the Ethereum ETF is no longer just a shadow asset following price fluctuations, but a truly cash-generating financial product. Looking at the longer term, as this model is validated, it's possible that traditional asset management giants like BlackRock and Fidelity will follow suit, potentially injecting hundreds of billions of dollars of long-term allocation funds into Ethereum. II. Record-High Staking Ratios and the Disappearance of the "Exit Queue" If ETF returns are more of a narrative breakthrough, then changes in the total staking ratio and the staking queue more directly reflect the behavior of funds themselves. First, the Ethereum staking ratio has reached a new all-time high. According to data from The Block, more than 36 million ETH are currently staked on the Ethereum Beacon Chain, accounting for nearly 30% of the network's circulating supply, with a staked market value exceeding $118 billion, setting a new record. The previous record for the highest percentage of circulating supply was 29.54%, which occurred in July 2025. From a supply and demand perspective, the large amount of ETH being staked means that it has temporarily withdrawn from the free market, indicating that a significant portion of the circulating ETH is shifting from a high-frequency trading asset to a long-term allocation asset playing a functional role. In other words, ETH is no longer just gas, a medium of exchange, or a speculative tool, but is assuming the role of a "means of production"—it participates in the network's operation through staking and continuously generates returns. Meanwhile, intriguing changes have also occurred in the validator queue. As of this writing, the Ethereum PoS staking exit queue is nearly empty, while the staking queue continues to grow (over 2.73 million ETH). In short, a large amount of ETH is currently being locked up in the system for the long term. Unlike trading, staking is a low-liquidity, long-term allocation method that emphasizes stable returns. The fact that funds are willing to re-enter the staking queue indicates at least one thing: at this stage, more and more participants are willing to accept the opportunity cost of this long-term lock-up. If we consider the distribution of institutional ETF returns, record-high staking ratios, and changes in the staking structure together, a relatively clear trend emerges: Ethereum staking is evolving from an early-stage on-chain participant incentive into a TradFi structured yield layer that is gradually being accepted by the traditional financial system and re-evaluated by long-term capital. Individually, none of these factors alone are sufficient to constitute a trend judgment, but together, they are outlining the gradual maturation of the Ethereum staking economy. III. The Future of Accelerated Maturation of the Staking Market However, this does not mean that staking has made ETH a "risk-free asset." On the contrary, with changes in the participant structure, the types of risks faced by staking are shifting. Technological risks are gradually being absorbed, while structural risks, liquidity risks, and the cost of understanding the mechanism are becoming more important. As is well known, in the last regulatory cycle, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) frequently wielded its big stick, taking enforcement action against several liquidity staking-related projects, including filing unregistered securities charges against MetaMask/Consensys, Lido/stETH, and Rocket Pool/rETH. This also brought uncertainty to the long-term development of Ethereum ETFs. From a practical perspective, whether and how ETFs participate in staking is essentially more of a product process and compliance structure design issue than a denial of the Ethereum network itself. As more institutions explore the boundaries in practice, the market is voting with real funds. For example, BitMine has staked over 1 million ETH in Ethereum PoS, reaching 1.032 million ETH, worth approximately $3.215 billion, representing a quarter of its total ETH holdings (4.143 million ETH). In short, Ethereum staking has evolved beyond a niche activity within the geek community. When ETFs begin to distribute stable returns, when long-term funds are willing to wait 45 days to enter the consensus layer, and when 30% of ETH is used as a security barrier, we are witnessing Ethereum officially building a native yield system accepted by global capital markets. Understanding this change itself is perhaps just as important as participating.

data.title: Solana Mobile Rolls Out SKR Airdrop, Turning Seeker Users Into Stakeholders
data.description:
From Smartphone Users to Token Holders: How the SKR Airdrop Works

Solana Mobile has officially launched its SKR token airdrop, opening claims to Seeker smartphone users and early developers in its decentralized app ecosystem. Announced on Jan. 21, the move marks a major milestone in the company’s push to build an open, community-driven mobile platform where users participate directly in governance and incentives.

静かに見てるエアドロップ勢へ 👀$SKR が近づいていて、Seeker ユーザーはスナップショット対象っぽい。
早めの行動は大事。

👉 https://t.co/Gdsa3U9OsW#SKR#エアドロップ#Solanapic.twitter.com/xngerMj7xp

— Maruf Makanmoto (@0x_CryptHeresy) January 20, 2026

More than 100,000 users are eligible to claim SKR. Seeker phone owners can access their allocation directly through the device’s Seed Vault Wallet by navigating to the Activity Tracking tab and completing the claim process. A small SOL balance is required to cover network fees.

The claim window remains open for 90 days, after which any unclaimed tokens will be returned to the airdrop pool. Developers are also included in the distribution, with teams that shipped approved apps to the Solana Mobile dApp Store during Season 1 able to claim SKR via the Publishing Portal. In total, roughly 2 billion tokens, or about 20% of the fixed 10 billion supply, have been allocated to users and developers in this initial phase.

What SKR Does: Governance, Staking, and the Solana Mobile Economy

SKR serves as the governance and utility backbone of the Solana Mobile ecosystem. The token underpins staking, community governance, app discovery and curation, and a device security framework known as Guardians, which is designed to enhance on-chain safety for mobile users.

Staking is already live, allowing holders to delegate their SKR and earn rewards. The token follows a linear inflation schedule, with issuance events occurring every 48 hours. Inflation begins at 10% annually, declining by 25% each year until it stabilizes at 2%, a structure designed to reward early participation while maintaining long-term supply discipline.

$SKR solana seeker token cookin’ pic.twitter.com/0uP0Me0z3K

— Lartiste (@Lartistokrat) January 21, 2026

SKR operates on Solana’s layer-1 blockchain and is positioned as a core economic asset for mobile-native Web3 applications, tying user activity directly to ecosystem incentives.

Solana’s Bigger Mobile Bet

The SKR launch coincides with the rollout of Seeker Season 2, which introduces new apps, rewards, and activity-based incentives across DeFi, gaming, payments, trading, and decentralized physical infrastructure networks (DePIN).

Seeker is the Android-based successor to Solana Mobile’s Saga phone and ships with blockchain-native features such as hardware-backed key storage, a native Solana dApp Store, and a Genesis NFT that unlocks future airdrops, exclusive content, and SKR-linked rewards.

Seeker | Solana Mobile combines mobile innovation and crypto technology.https://t.co/JcFlLP5MbN

On solana, the $SKR token stands out as a strong coin with an airdrop on the horizon.$SOL$FARTCOIN#SOL#Solanapic.twitter.com/wVEdFC1y4k

— howshename🥰 (@javecols) January 19, 2026

SKR is now tradable on major Solana decentralized exchanges including Raydium, Jupiter, Orca, and Meteora, as well as centralized platforms such as Crypto.com, Bybit, KuCoin, Gate.io, and Bitget. The token was trading around $0.010–$0.0106 at publication, with a market capitalization of roughly $56.6 million and a fully diluted valuation near $99 million.

Solana Mobile previously reported around 150,000 Seeker preorders across more than 50 countries, positioning the device as a cornerstone of on-chain mobile adoption. With SKR now live, the company is betting that ownership, not just usability, will define the next generation of mobile platforms.

data.title: Why did a "sell-off of US stocks" occur, and what impact will it have on the cryptocurrency market?
data.description:
Deng Tong, Jinse Finance

On January 21, 2026, the three major US stock indexes closed sharply lower. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 1.76%, the S&P 500 fell 2.06%, marking its largest single-day drop since October of last year, and the Nasdaq Composite fell 2.39%. Technology stocks generally fell, with Oracle and Broadcom falling more than 5%, Nvidia and Tesla falling more than 4%, and Amazon and Apple falling more than 3%. Market capitalization evaporated by more than $1.3 trillion in a single day.

The trend of "selling America" ​​has suddenly emerged.

I. Why has a "sell America" ​​situation emerged?

"Sell America" ​​trading refers to a market strategy in which investors simultaneously reduce their holdings of core dollar assets such as US stocks, US bonds, and the US dollar, and even short-sell dollar-related assets through derivatives, in order to avoid risks such as US macroeconomic policies, fiscal sustainability, and monetary credit. This is often accompanied by a shift of funds to traditional safe-haven assets such as gold and non-US currencies.

Tom Nakamura, head of fixed income and foreign exchange at Canadian investment firm AGF Investments, said the recent volatility in US Treasuries stemmed from two catalysts: First, the controversy surrounding Trump's pursuit of Greenland from Denmark triggered new tariff threats, exacerbating short-term growth concerns and raising expectations for policy easing, while amplifying long-term inflation and fiscal risks, pushing the yield curve steeper; second, the Japanese factor was equally crucial, with the market focused on whether Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi would introduce large-scale stimulus policies. 1. Geopolitical and Trade Tensions Trump covets Greenland and has demonstrated a tough stance. On January 21, US President Trump stated that his goal of controlling Greenland "will never change" and refused to rule out the possibility of taking Greenland by force. Trump clarified his purpose on social media: to seize sovereignty over Greenland from NATO ally Denmark. The previous day, in an interview, Trump refused to comment on whether he would use force to seize Greenland, saying "no comment." In response to Trump's "explosive acquisition," the Danish pension fund Akademiker Pension will exit the US Treasury market. As of the end of December, the pension fund held $100 million in US Treasury bonds. Its chief investment officer stated that the US is "basically no longer a good credit destination." Going back further, Trump even resorted to tariffs against several European countries in his attempt to acquire Greenland. On January 17th local time, US President Trump announced via social media that due to the Greenland issue, starting February 1st, all goods exported to the US from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the UK, the Netherlands, and Finland would be subject to a 10% tariff. By June 1st, the tariff would increase to 25%. This tariff would continue to be paid until an agreement was reached on the "complete and total purchase of Greenland." In his post, Trump stated that these eight countries "have all gone to Greenland, for reasons unknown. Strong measures must be taken to end this potentially dangerous situation swiftly and decisively in order to protect global peace and security." Trump also stated, "The United States has been working towards this deal for over 150 years. Many presidents have tried, and with good reason, but Denmark has consistently refused. Now, the need to acquire Greenland is particularly urgent due to the Golden Dome defense system and modern offensive and defensive weapons systems." In response to President Trump's tariff threats, eight European countries issued a joint statement on the 18th, stating that the threat of additional tariffs damages transatlantic relations and risks triggering a dangerous vicious cycle. The eight countries will respond with "unity and coordination." Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, the Netherlands, Norway, Sweden, and the United Kingdom jointly signed the statement. The statement said, "We stand firmly with the Kingdom of Denmark and the people of Greenland… We are willing to engage in dialogue based on the principles of sovereignty and territorial integrity." The eight countries will continue to respond to the US tariff threats in a "united and coordinated" manner, and "we are firmly committed to safeguarding our sovereignty." The statement also addressed Trump's accusations regarding the "Arctic Endurance" military exercise, explaining that the exercise was pre-coordinated by Denmark and its allies, aimed at strengthening Arctic security, and aligned with shared transatlantic interests, posing "no threat to anyone." Trump has already announced a 200% tariff on French wine and champagne, and French President Macron will join the peace committee. Following Trump's tariff threats, the EU is preparing to suspend the EU-US trade agreement, citing US President Donald Trump's vow to impose tariffs on countries supporting Greenland. Manfred Weber, chairman of the European People's Party (EPP), the largest political group in the European Parliament, said on Saturday that an agreement with the US is no longer possible. "The EPP supports the EU-US trade agreement, but given Trump's threats regarding Greenland, approval is impossible at this stage," Weber posted on social media, adding that the EU's agreement to lower tariffs on "US products" "must be suspended." The EU-US trade agreement was reached last summer by European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and Trump. Parts of the agreement are already in effect, but it still requires formal approval from the European Parliament. If EPP lawmakers join forces with left-leaning political groups, they may have enough votes to delay or veto its ratification. Pepperstone analyst Michael Brown stated that Trump's new tariff threats against several European countries mark a sharp escalation in his efforts to acquire Greenland. The market reaction was largely in line with expectations, with stock index futures falling across the board, the dollar weakening in a mild "sell-on-America" ​​trade, and precious metal prices surging. Despite numerous uncertainties and the need to closely monitor European reactions, Brown believes this is Trump's old trick: first issue a dramatic threat, then escalate it significantly, forcing concessions and ultimately reaching some kind of agreement. For the market, this could mean some short-term volatility as news noise becomes deafening, but a rebound is expected when another "TACO" moment arrives.
